definition: Hans Jonas (1903–1993), a German philosopher, authored *The Gnostic Religion*, a foundational text that revolutionized Philip K. Dick's self-understanding. Upon reading Jonas in the late 1970s, Dick experienced a profound shock of recognition: the bizarre, idiosyncratic cosmology he had spent years attempting to codify in the Exegesis mapped almost perfectly onto classical first-century Sethian and Valentinian Gnosticism. Jonas's rigorous academic explanation of the Gnostic 'alien God', the blind Demiurge, the 'prison' of the material world, and the awakening spark of *pneuma* provided Dick with the exact historical and theological template to legitimize his 2-3-74 visions.
